title: "Emerald Labyrinth Puzzle v11573"

description: |
  At 22:10 the Emerald Labyrinth Jewel vanished from its locked display.
  Four badge-holders (1, 2, 3, 4) were recorded in the vault corridor.

  - Exactly one suspect lies in all their statements; the other three are perfectly truthful.
  - Exactly one suspect is guilty (the thief).
  - The universal liar and the thief must be different individuals.

  Suspect 1
    (1) “If suspect 2 is the liar, then I (1) am guilty.”
    (2) “Suspect 2 was indeed at the display at 22:10.”
    (3) “Suspect 3 is not the liar.”
    (4) “Suspect 4 was also present at 22:10.”

  Suspect 2
    (1) “I was not near the display at 22:10.”
    (2) “Suspect 1 is innocent.”
    (3) “Suspect 3 is the liar.”
    (4) “If suspect 4 was present, then suspect 1 must be lying.”

  Suspect 3
    (1) “Suspect 1 is definitely guilty.”
    (2) “Suspect 2 is the liar.”
    (3) “Suspect 2 was indeed present at 22:10.”
    (4) “Suspect 4 was present as well.”

  Suspect 4
    (1) “Suspect 1 is the thief.”
    (2) “Suspect 2 is the liar.”
    (3) “Suspect 3 is not the liar.”
    (4) “I too was at the display at 22:10.”

question: "Who stole the Emerald Labyrinth Jewel? Return a single digit (1–4)."

answer: >
  explanation: |
    Assume $L1$.

    Then $1$–$(1)$ is false: "If $L2$ then $G1$".  
    To be false, $L2$ must be true and $G1$ must be false.  
    That makes $L1$ and $L2$ both true — contradiction.  
    So $L1$ is invalid.

    Assume $L2$.

    $2$–$(1)$ is false $\Rightarrow P2$ is true  
    $2$–$(2)$ is false $\Rightarrow G1$ is true  
    $2$–$(3)$ is false $\Rightarrow L3$ is false  
    $2$–$(4)$ is false $\Rightarrow$ antecedent $P4$ true and conclusion $L1$ false ⇒ implication is false

    Suspect $1$:

    $1$–$(1):$ if $L2$ then $G1$, both true ⇒ statement is true  
    $1$–$(2):$ $P2$ is true ⇒ statement is true  
    $1$–$(3):$ $L3$ is false ⇒ "3 is not liar" is true  
    $1$–$(4):$ $P4$ is true ⇒ statement is true

    Suspect $3$:

    $3$–$(1):$ $G1$ is true ⇒ "1 is guilty" is true  
    $3$–$(2):$ $L2$ is true ⇒ "2 is the liar" is true  
    $3$–$(3):$ $P2$ is true  
    $3$–$(4):$ $P4$ is true

    Suspect $4$:

    $4$–$(1):$ $G1$ is true  
    $4$–$(2):$ $L2$ is true  
    $4$–$(3):$ $L3$ is false ⇒ "3 is not the liar" is true  
    $4$–$(4):$ $P4$ is true

    Exactly one liar ($L2$), exactly one thief ($G1$), and they are different

  final: '1'
